title: 7 Managing Incidents in Enterprise Fraud Management
body_text:
7	Managing Incidents in Enterprise Fraud Management
7.1	Introduction to Managing Incidents in Enterprise Fraud Management
When the system detects a suspicious transaction, an alert is created for that transaction within an incident. Incidents are displayed in the Open list on the Incidents page. EFM users must investigate alerted transactions within incidents and identify the alerts as Risk or No-risk. See Chapter 8, Managing Transaction Alerts, for procedures.
Note:
Training videos
are available for the procedures in this chapter.
7.2	Accessing the Incidents Page
To access the Incidents page:
Log in to EFM as described in Section 5.2, Logging in to Enterprise Fraud Management, then select
Incidents
on the Dashboard page.
Select a list to view specific incidents. The Incidents page defaults to the Open list, which displays unreviewed incidents with alerted transactions. You can also view incidents in other lists. See Figure 1.
â
Referred to me
â Shows unreviewed incidents that were referred to you or a team youâre a member of. Only you or applicable team members can see these incidents.
â¡
All Incidents
â Lets you filter the list of incidents using pre-created filters (e.g., AML Rules, Customers Only). See Section 7.7, Using Incident Filters, for more information.
â¢
Open
â Shows all unreviewed incidents with alerted transactions.
â£
Pending
â Shows incidents that you have specifically sent to this list. Use this list to monitor suspicious customer activity thatâs not decisively Risk or No-risk. All EFM users in your organization can see incidents in the Pending list.
â¤
Closed
â
Shows incidents with alerted transactions that were identified and reported as Risk or No-risk.
Figure 1: Incident Lists in Sidebar
Related Information
Training Videos:
Fraud Alert Services in EFM
7.3	Overview of Incidents on the Incidents Page
This section describes how to read and navigate through incidents on the Incidents page. See Figure 2.
â
Identification
â Transit number and your customerâs account number.
â¡
Alerts
â Indicates how many unreviewed alerts are in this incident.
â¢
Highest Score
â shows incidents where the highest score assigned to an alert in that incident is between the values you set. The risk prioritization bar displays the colour green to indicate low risk, amber to indicate moderate risk, and red to indicate high risk. The risk score by itself doesnât determine whether a transaction is fraudulent. When used alongside our rules-based system, it improves detection accuracy and efficiency. The score offers an additional layer of insight, helping to identify patterns and prioritize higher-risk transactions for review. This is only applicable to Interac
Â®
e-Transfer transactions.
â£
Event Type
â Displays the type of event.
â¤
Last Alert Time
â Displays the date and time of the most recently triggered alert.
â¥
Viewing
â Displays the name of the user who is currently viewing this incident (if applicable). An incident is locked when another user is viewing it. To access an incident that another user is viewing, send them a message and ask them to exit. Alternatively, select the
x
after the userâs name is displayed in the Viewing column. This exits the user and unlocks the incident.
â¦
Last Review Time
â Displays the date and time of when the transaction was last reviewed.
â§
Updated By
â Displays the name of the user who last updated the transaction.
â¨
eTransfer Type
â Displays the type of payment (e.g., Interac
34). The eTransfer Type is only applicable to Interac
e-Transfer transactions.
â©
Referred To
â Displays the name of the user or team assigned to review the incident (if applicable).
âª
Rules
â
The rules that triggered the alerts. See Section 7.8, Viewing Incidents in the Open List, for how to view a description of a triggered rule.
â«
Tags
â Additional information related to the rules that triggered an alert. See Section 7.4, Tags, for a list and description of tags used in EFM.
Figure 2: Incident List Layout
Select the Sidebar icon beside the Central 1 logo to expand or hide the sidebar. See Figure 3.
Figure 3: Expanding or Hiding the Sidebar
Select the
+
sign beside Identification to expand all the incidents on the page. Alternatively, select the down arrow beside an incident to expand that row. The rows are colour-coded alternately to differentiate between incidents. See Figure 4.
Figure 4: Expanding all Incidents on a Page
When one or more incidents are selected, you can click
Select all items
or
Select none
to
select or deselect incidents. See Figure 5.
Figure 5: Selecting or Deselecting Incidents
Select one or more incidents to activate bulk
Actions
. This allows you to select an action from the drop-down menu and apply it to the selected incidents. See Figure 6.
Note:	For âDelete Selectedâ, the system will prompt you to confirm the action. Incidents that are deleted canât be retrieved.
Figure 6: Actions for Selected Incidents
Select
Column Settings
to select the columns you want to display. Deselect columns you want to hide. Select the up or down arrows in the Sorting
section
to change the order of the displayed incidents. Alternatively, you can select the column headings in the Incident List to sort the incidents. See Figure 7.
Figure 7: Column Settings
Related Information
Training Videos:
Fraud Alert Services in EFM
7.4	Tags
Tags, or reason codes, are descriptive tags used to explain why EFMâs machine learning model has identified a transactionâs behaviour as suspicious. The tags are generated by evaluating how a group of similar model attributes contribute to the risk score. The tags are generated if the model attributes exceed a specific threshold. For example, the machine learning model analyzes different pieces of information regarding a customerâs behaviour. If the model notices something unusual, a tag for âUnusual Customer Transactionâ could be triggered. Tags are also simplifications of the machine learning modelâs decisions and are meant to help guide users when theyâre reviewing alerted transactions.
The table below provides a list and description of all tags used in EFM.
Tag or Reason Code Abbreviation and Name
Description
Examples
Sa
Suspicious Amount
The transaction value ($ value) is outside the normal range of transaction values for a specific customer, account, device, recipient/counterparty, etc.
A customer who has never sent more than $500 in a single transaction makes a single transaction worth $10,000 (indicating that the transaction is out-of-character).
Sev
Suspicious Entity Volume
The number of transactions involving group of customers, account, device, recipient/counterparty, etc., is unusually large.
Thereâs an increase in the number of devices that are associated with the customer (there are various transactions flowing from multiple devices).
Scb
Suspicious Customer Behaviour
The customerâs transaction patterns resemble those commonly associated with fraudulent activity.
Additional Note
: This reason code is looking at broader patterns across a given customer or account (e.g., age of customer/account, recency of activity, transaction payments made over different time periods, etc.)
A new customer sends a series of transactions to a single beneficiary (potentially to launder money from a hidden source).
Sab
Suspicious Account Behaviour
The accountâs transaction patterns resemble those commonly associated with fraudulent activity.
A newly opened account sends a large transaction immediately after being created.
Scpb
Suspicious Counterparty Behaviour
The recipient/counterparty of the transaction is known to be involved in many fraudulent transactions or has behaviour associated with an elevated risk of fraud.
A customer sends a transaction to a gambling service that is regularly reported to defraud its customers.
Sei
Suspicious Entity Interaction
The interaction between a customer and another entity (e.g., recipient/counterparty, phone number or email used) displays a risky pattern of behaviour.
A customer sends a large volume of transactions to a recipient/counterparty with whom they had no previous history.
Uct
Unusual Customer Transaction
The details of the transaction (e.g., how recently the transaction occurred after a previous action, or customer information) doesnât match the customerâs usual patterns.
A sudden change in phone number used by the customer elevates the risk that the transaction isnât legitimate.
Hvv
High Value Velocity
A large volume of high-value transactions are being sent by the customer.
Five transactions worth over $2,000 are sent from an account within a single hour.
Stt
Suspicious Transaction Time
The time of transaction is unusual for the customer.
A customer makes a large transaction at 4am local time.
Please note:
An entity is a unique factor that is tracked over time (e.g., customer, account, device, recipient/counterparty, phone number, etc.).
Several reason codes can be provided for a single transaction.
Itâs possible that the model score doesnât trigger an alert, but a reason code is produced.
Itâs possible that two events appear similar to each other but donât receive the same reason code (unlike how rules work). This is because the machine learning modelâs score prediction combines information from all fields.
The machine learning model doesnât always produce a reason code. This occurs if the model score is low or if no single factor stands out enough to explain the decision. For example, even with a high model score (if many small factors from different areas contribute to that score), there isnât enough to trigger a reason code.
7.5	Using the Filters Sidebar in the Open Incident List
The Filters sidebar allows you to filter incidents by the following:
Date range
â shows incidents where the latest alert in that incident was generated between the selected dates and times.
Rules
â show incidents where at least one alert was triggered by the selected rules.
Highest score
â shows incidents where the highest score assigned to an alert in that incident is between the values you set. The risk prioritization bar displays the colour green to indicate low risk, amber to indicate moderate risk, and red to indicate high risk. The risk score by itself doesnât determine whether a transaction is fraudulent. When used alongside our rules-based system, it improves detection accuracy and efficiency. The score offers an additional layer of insight, helps to identify patterns, and prioritizes higher-risk transactions for review. This is only applicable to Interac
e-Transfer transactions.
To open the filters sidebar:
Log in to EFM as described in Section 5.2, Logging in to Enterprise Fraud Management, then select
Incidents
on the Dashboard page.
Select
Filters
. See Figure 8.
Figure 8: Filters Sidebar Button
To create a filter in the sidebar:
â
Select a start date from the drop-down calendar in the From
field. See Figure 9.
â¡
Select the clock, then select a start time.
â¢
Select an end date from the drop-down calendar in the To
field.
â£
Select the clock, then select an end time.
â¤
Enter the name of the rule in the Rules search box. The results will be filtered as you type.
Note:	You can only filter on rules that were added at the Tenant level (your financial institution). The filters sidebar doesnât allow filtering on universal rules added at the Landlord level.
â¥
Alternatively, select the check box next to specific rules. Incidents that were triggered by the selected rules and that donât match any other filter will be shown.
â¦
Select the box if you want to filter by the highest score.
â§
Use the sliders to set the minimum and maximum risk score or enter the minimum and maximum risk scores in the boxes on the left
and
right of the sliders. Incidents with a âRiskiest Scoreâ (i.e., highest risk value for any alert in that incident) that is outside this range wonât be shown.
â¨
Select
x
to close the filter sidebar.
â©
Select
Reset
to delete filters created.
Figure 9: Filters Sidebar Settings
The
Filters
button changes colour when you create a filter. You can create multiple filters with different search criteria. The filters you create are displayed under the Incidents heading. See Figure 10.
Figure 10: Filter Created
To remove filters:
Select the
x
beside the applied filter(s) listed under the Incidents heading, or
Open the filters sidebar and select
Reset
.
Related Information
Training Videos:
Fraud Alert Services in EFM
7.6	Using the Filters Sidebar in the Closed Incidents List
The filters sidebar in the closed Incident list allows you to filter for Risk and No-risk transactions within closed incidents. You can search on Alert time and on Review time.
Note:	This upgrade only applies to incidents and events that occur after May 14, 2023.
To open Filters in the Closed incidents sidebar:
Log in to EFM as described in Section 5.2, Logging in to Enterprise Fraud Management, then select
Incidents
on the Dashboard page.
Go to
Closed
incidents, then select
Filters
. See Figure 11.
Figure 11: Selecting Filters in Closed Incidents
To create a filter:
Note:	Steps 1 to 4 apply to the Alert time and to the Review time.
â
Select a start date from the drop-down calendar in the From
field. See Figure 12.
â¡
Select the clock, then select a start time (optional).
â¢
Select an end date from the drop-down calendar in the To
field.
â£
Select the clock, then select an end time (optional).
â¤
Select an Alert status (Risk or No-risk)
â¥
Enter the name of the rule in the Rules search box. The results will be filtered as you type.
Note:	You can only filter on rules that were added at the Tenant level (your financial institution). The filters sidebar doesnât allow filtering on universal rules added at the Landlord level.
â¦
Alternatively, select the check box next to specific rules. Incidents that were triggered by the selected rules and that donât match any other filter will be shown.
Figure 12: Creating a Filter
â
Incidents that match filtered results are displayed under Incidents. See Figure 13.
â¡
Select
x
to delete individual filters.
â¢
Select
Reset
to clear all filters.
Figure 13: Filtered Results
7.7	Using Incident Filters
You can activate an incident filter on any incident list page to filter incidents that match pre-defined conditions. An active incident filter applies to all the Incident lists (Referred to me, Open, Pending, and Closed). See Section 9.1, Creating Incident Filters, for procedures.
To activate an incident filter:
Log in to EFM as described in Section 5.2, Logging in to Enterprise Fraud Management, then select
Incidents
on the Dashboard page.
Select the filter from the drop-down menu in the page sidebar. See Figure 14.
Figure 14: Selecting an Incident Filter
The number of incidents in each list that matches the active filter is shown beside the name of the list. See Figure 15.
Figure 15: Filtered Incident Based on Activated Filter
Related Information
Training Videos:
Fraud Alert Services in EFM
7.8	Viewing Incidents in the Open List
The Open list contains incidents with unreviewed alerts that relate to a customerâs transactions.
When you select an incident, you can perform the following:
Investigate the details of an alert and identify it as Risk or No-risk
Add the incident to the Pending list
Refer the incident to another user or team
Add the incident to a list (e.g., a negative list or data list created by your financial institution)
To view an incident:
Log in to EFM as described in Section 5.2, Logging in to Enterprise Fraud Management, then select
Incidents
on the Dashboard page.
Select a customer ID in the Identification column. See Figure 16.
Figure 16: Selecting an Incident
This opens the review page with the Activity Timeline for that incident. The Activity tab shows each alert, other events, and any activity from other users. See Figure 17.
â
Details
â Displays a list of the triggers for this alert.
â¡
Time
â
Shows when the transaction came in.
â¢
Risk Score
â The risk prioritization bar and risk score that EFM generates. The risk prioritization bar displays the colour green to indicate low risk, amber to indicate moderate risk, and red to indicate high risk. The risk score by itself doesnât determine whether a transaction is fraudulent. When used alongside our rules-based system, it improves detection accuracy and efficiency. The score offers an additional layer of insight, helps identify patterns, and prioritizes higher-risk transactions for review. This is only applicable to Interac
e-Transfer transactions.
â£
Activity
â Displays
the event type (e.g., paymentRT, which is the real-time fraud check against a payment; or paymentNRT, which indicates that a payment has gone through to Interac) and the reason for the alert (i.e., the rule that triggered the alert).
â¤
Transaction ID
â A unique identifier for each transaction.
â¥
Amount
â Shows the amount of the transaction.
â¦
eTransfer Type
â Displays the version of Interac used for the transaction.
â§
RSA IP Address
â Displays the IP address associated with the transaction.
â¨
Direction
â Shows the direction of the transaction (e.g., Inbound, Outbound).
â©
Msg Status
â Shows the status of the message (e.g., New, Corrected, Cancelled, Fraud, Resubmitted).
âª
Msg Type
â States the type of message (e.g., Payment.Send_eTransfer).
â«
Add comment
â Used to provide information on the status of the investigation (e.g., waiting on details from customer).
â¬
Activity filter
â Used to filter specific activities you want to view.
â­
Search
â Used to search for any attribute related to the original event (e.g., customer ID, debit account number, email address).
â®
Back to List
â Takes you back to the Open Incidents list.
Figure 17: Viewing Actions on the Activity Timeline
Select the eye icon in the Details column to open the event details sidebar. You can see a summary of the alert information (rules and tags). You can also scroll down the sidebar to see additional associated information (e.g., From ID, Account ID, Sender Email). See Figure 18.
Figure 18: Viewing Event Details Sidebar
In the event details sidebar, you can toggle between alerts if there is more than one alert. You can also navigate to the scoring models sidebar that provides additional information on the event related to the alert. See Figure 19.
â
Select the direction keys to toggle between alerts to see the business rules that apply to each alert.
â¡
Select the eye icon to see additional event details in the scoring models sidebar.
â¢
Select
x
to close the sidebar.
Figure 19: Viewing and Navigating the Event Sidebar
In the scoring models sidebar, you can toggle between alerts if there is more than one alert, and you can toggle between both sidebars. See Figure 20.
â
Select the direction keys to toggle between alerts and see the tags that apply to each alert.
â¡
Select the eye icon to toggle between both sidebars.
â¢
Select
x
to close the sidebar.
Figure 20: Viewing and Navigating the Scoring Models Sidebar
The Details tab provides a summary of the eventâs history (e.g., status of alerts, rules, and tags). See Figure 21.
Figure 21: Viewing Summary of Eventâs History
While on the review page of an incident, hover your mouse over a rule to display a description of the rule. See Figure 22.
Figure 22: Viewing Rule Descriptions
Related Information
Training Videos:
Fraud Alert Services in EFM
7.9	Using the Activity Filter in an Open Incident
You can use the Activity filter to filter specific activities (e.g., unalerted transactions) or events (e.g., paymentRT, paymentNRT, wireRT, chequeRT).
To filter activities on the incident review page:
Log in to EFM as described in Section 5.2, Logging in to Enterprise Fraud Management, then select
Incidents
on the Dashboard page.
Select a customer ID in the Identification column as described in Section 7.8, Viewing Incidents in the Open List.
Select
Activity filter
. See Figure 23.
Figure 23: Selecting the Activity Filter
You can filter on any of the Activities or event types displayed and you can export analyst actions to a PDF. See Figure 24.
Note:	When youâve made your selections, click anywhere on the page to close the panel and view the results.
â
Activity
â Select the activities you want to see. To hide an activity, de-select it to remove the highlight.
â¡
Event Types
â Select the events you want to see. Only event types in bold font occur in that incident. In the example below, paymentNRT and paymentRT are the only two events that occur in this incident.
â¢
Search
â Use this field to search for a specific event type.
â£
Analyst Actions
â Any analyst actions that occur in the incident can be exported.
â¤
Export Selected Actions
â Select this button to export analyst actions to a PDF. If there arenât any analyst actions in the incident, the Export Selected Actions button remains inactive.
Note:	The time zone displayed in the PDF for exported actions is set to UTC (Coordinated Universal Time).
â¥
Reset Filter
â Select this link to set the Activity filter back to default. The default does not include filtering non-alerted transactions. If youâre searching for non-alerted transactions, you must select the term to highlight it.
Figure 24: Using the Activity Filter
The dots on the right side of the incidentâs review page are colour-coded to indicate what they represent. See Figure 25.
Figure 25: Description of Colour-Coded Dots
Note:	While there is an option to select âUnder investigationâ as an activity, it does not affect any of your activity filter requests. This activity solution is not available to clients. If all activities are selected (including the âUnder investigationâ activity), the colour of the âActivity Filterâ text will be the colour black. Otherwise, if none or only some of the activities are selected, the âActivity filterâ text will reflect the colour pink instead. See Figure 26.
Figure 26: Under investigation Activity
Related Information
Training Videos:
Fraud Alert Services in EFM
7.10	Referring Incidents
You can refer an incident to other users or teams for analysis. This allows the reviewing workload to be divided between users or re-assigned to users with review specialties.
A referred incident displays the referred user or team in the âReferred toâ column of the incident list, and the incident will be added to the âReferred to meâ incident list of the corresponding user (or team members).
To refer an incident:
Log in to EFM as described in Section 5.2, Logging in to Enterprise Fraud Management, then select
Incidents
on the Dashboard page.
Select the incident. See Figure 27.
Figure 27: Selecting an Incident
Complete the actions in the sidebar on the Incidentâs review page. See Figure 28.
â
Select
Refer
.
â¡
Refer to
â Select a user or team from the drop-down menu.
â¢
Comment
(strongly recommended) â Enter a comment or select one from the template drop-down menu. Ensure comments are appropriate for EFM users and customers, clear, succinct, and descriptive. A comment canât exceed 400 characters. Add an additional comment if needed.
Important
:	Comments are visible on other downstream systems such as the Interac eâTransfer Online Administration System (OAS) or other banking applications. Comments must be appropriate for both user and customer audiences.
â£
Select
Refer incident
.
Figure 28: Sending the Incident Referral
The incident is added to the âReferred to meâ list of the user or team you selected, and only they can see the incidents in the âReferred to meâ list.
Related Information
Training Videos:
Fraud Alert Services in EFM
7.11	Sending Incidents to Pending List
You can send an incident to the Pending list to postpone a review, for example, until further information has been gathered.
To send an incident to the Pending list:
Log in to EFM as described in Section 5.2, Logging in to Enterprise Fraud Management, then select
Incidents
on the Dashboard page.
Select the incident. See Figure 29.
Figure 29: Selecting Incident for Pending List
Complete the actions in the sidebar on the Incidentâs review page. See Figure 30.
â
Select
Pending
.
â¡
Comment
(strongly recommended) â Enter a comment or select one from the template drop-down menu. Ensure comments are appropriate for EFM users and customers, clear, succinct, and descriptive. A comment canât exceed 400 characters. Add an additional comment if needed.
Important
:	Comments are visible on other downstream systems such as the Interac eâTransfer Online Administration System (OAS) or other banking applications. Comments must be appropriate for both user and customer audiences.
â¢
Select
Add to Pending
.
Figure 30: Sending Incident to Pending List
The incident is added to the Pending List. All users in your organization can see incidents in the Pending list.
Related Information
Training Videos:
Fraud Alert Services in EFM
7.12	Adding Incidents to a Data List
Universal watch lists are already on the system. But in addition, you can use internal data lists to collect specific values from incidents (e.g., suspicious emails). See Section 9.2, Creating Internal Data Lists, for procedures on how to create your own data lists.
To add an incident to an internal data list:
Log in to EFM as described in Section 5.2, Logging in to Enterprise Fraud Management, then select
Incidents
on the Dashboard page.
Select the incident. See Figure 31.
Figure 31: Selecting Incident
Complete the actions in the sidebar on the Incidentâs review page. See Figure 32.
â
Select
Add to List
.
â¡
Select the list from the drop-down menu that you want to add data to. The values that will be added to the selected list are displayed.
â¢
You can remove any of the values you donât want to add to the selected data list.
â£
Comment
(strongly recommended) â Enter a comment or select one from the template drop-down menu. Ensure comments are appropriate for EFM users and customers, clear, succinct, and descriptive. A comment canât exceed 400 characters. Add an additional comment if needed.
Important
:	Comments are visible on other downstream systems such as the Interac eâTransfer Online Administration System (OAS) or other banking applications. Comments must be appropriate for both user and customer audiences.
â¤
Select
Add to List
.
Figure 32: Adding Incident to Internal Data List
END
